judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are strong and nearly identical in substance on points 1-3, but Report 2 explicitly performs a concrete reproducibility check (re-running the suite twice to confirm determinism) in addition to naming the set-ordering instability source, while Report 1 only names the instability source without a concrete repeat-verification step, making Report 2 the stronger answer overall.

report_1.evidence: they POST to a live endpoint (https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score) with Bearer ${SCORELAB_API_KEY}... Enabling them on the default run would (a) incur per-call API cost on every test run... The teammate's "enable the live marker so nothing is skipped" step should be rejected.

report_2.evidence: each one POSTs to the real model endpoint https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score with a SCORELAB_API_KEY bearer token... every default pytest invocation would make paid network calls (a recurring per-call bill)... The "2 deselected" line in the pytest output is intended behavior, not a gap to fix.

report_1.evidence: Running scripts/update_golden.py right now would bake that regression into the golden file... exactly the failure mode goldens exist to catch. The right sequence: revert the rounding change (or split it into its own reviewed commit if it's actually intended), hand-verify the expected new values (I computed them...)... then run scripts/update_golden.py, inspect the resulting golden diff against those hand-computed numbers, and commit it alongside the code change

report_2.evidence: before refreshing tests/golden/expected_scores.json, every changed value must trace to the intended change, and right now it doesn't... the confidence changes (0.812→0.81, 0.444→0.44, 0.633→0.63) trace solely to the rounding change... That hunk is either unintended (revert it, then regenerate) or a second deliberate change (state and justify it in the PR, then regenerate) — it must not be silently laundered into the new project documentation.

report_1.evidence: Don't cite docs/testing.md — both of its numbers are wrong; source the figures from the repo itself... it has never been updated since the initial commit (git log --follow docs/testing.md shows only commit 63d3d72). Measured reality: grep shows exactly one test marked @pytest.mark.flaky... and running pyflakes... yields exactly 4 warnings

report_2.evidence: Flaky and lint reporting: measure, don't cite the doc — both of its numbers are wrong. docs/testing.md claims "Known flaky: 2 tests..." and "Lint warnings: 9." Measured directly: grep finds exactly one @pytest.mark.flaky test... running the linter... reports exactly 4 warnings

report_1.evidence: One caveat about the script itself: it iterates a set (CATEGORIES), so JSON key order is nondeterministic across runs (Python hash randomization) — harmless to correctness but a source of spurious diffs worth noting or fixing.

report_2.evidence: scripts/update_golden.py iterates a Python set (CATEGORIES) whose ordering is arbitrary under hash randomization (its own comment says so)... After regenerating, I would re-run the suite in plain assert mode at least twice to confirm the new project documentation is stable, and read the golden-file diff hunk by hunk before committing it.
